questions.question: Why is autocomplete usually more latency-sensitive than many normal search requests?
options: Because users generate many requests per query as they type.
Because autocomplete never allows caching.
Because every request must scan the full document corpus.
Because ranking is impossible without cross-region writes.
questions.explanation: Per-keystroke interaction amplifies latency and request rate.

questions.question: What is the main role of a prefix index in autocomplete?
options: To run full-text document ranking for each keypress.
To cheaply generate a small candidate set for a typed prefix.
To replace all policy filtering and moderation checks.
To guarantee globally fresh trends every second.
questions.explanation: The prefix index is about fast candidate lookup.

questions.question: Why is snapshot-based serving a common autocomplete design choice?
options: It gives predictable low-latency reads while isolating update work from the serving path.
It removes the need for ranking features entirely.
It guarantees that every result is personalized.
It prevents any suggestion from ever becoming hot.
questions.explanation: Stable snapshots simplify the serving path.

questions.question: Which metric best detects that update freshness is slipping?
options: Snapshot age by shard and publish success rate.
Median browser viewport width of clients.
The number of engineers on the ranking team.
Average string length of the top result.
questions.explanation: Snapshot age directly captures serving staleness.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with adding per-user personalization to autocomplete?
options: Higher relevance for some users but more memory pressure and feature-fetch complexity.
Lower latency and less memory use for all requests.
Guaranteed policy compliance without moderation systems.
No need for region-level popularity modeling.
questions.explanation: Personalization adds state and serving complexity.

questions.question: Why is a stale-safe fallback snapshot useful?
options: It keeps the system available when fresh ranking signals or publish steps are delayed.
It makes blue/green rollouts unnecessary.
It guarantees real-time trend discovery under all failures.
It removes the need for policy blocklists.
questions.explanation: Availability often matters more than perfect freshness.

questions.question: Which failure mode is most directly addressed by canary diffing during snapshot rollout?
options: A corrupted or bad-quality snapshot reaching all serving shards.
A browser typing slower than expected.
A user issuing the same prefix twice.
A data center changing its IP range.
questions.explanation: Canary diffing is a rollout-safety measure.

questions.question: What is the stronger explanation for separating candidate generation from ranking?
options: It limits expensive ranking logic to a small set instead of the whole corpus.
It avoids storing query popularity anywhere.
It guarantees exact consistency between all regions.
It eliminates the need for caches.
questions.explanation: The split bounds compute while preserving quality.
